notes: One of the most 'double-edged' words in Japanese. In formal contexts (exams, workplace instructions), it means 'appropriate' or 'proper.' However, in daily conversation, it often means 'careless' or 'just saying/doing whatever without thinking.' Context and tone are critical to determine if it is a compliment or a criticism.

grammar_points.usage: When modifying a noun, the meaning shifts based on the noun's nature. With technical nouns, it means 'suitable.' with communicative nouns (reply, talk), it usually implies 'half-baked' or 'vague.'
Used to tell someone to 'do it as you see fit' or 'don't stress over the details.' It can be a kind way to grant autonomy or a lazy way to delegate a task without instructions.

phrases.ja: 適当[てきとう]に返事[へんじ]をする
適当[てきとう]にあしらう
適当[てきとう]な場所[ばしょ]
適当[てきとう]に切[き]り上[あ]げる
phrases.en: to wrap up at a good point

phrases.ja: かなり適当[てきとう]な人[ひと]
適当[てきとう]に選[えら]ぶ

examples.ja: 自分[じぶん]のレベルに合[あ]った適当[てきとう]な教科書[きょうかしょ]を探[さが]している。
examples.en: I am looking for a suitable textbook that matches my level.

examples.ja: 彼[かれ]はいつもその場[ば]しのぎの適当[てきとう]なことばかり言[い]う。
examples.en: He's always just saying whatever random things to get through the moment.

examples.ja: 詳[くわ]しいルールは知[し]らないので、適当[てきとう]にやってみた。
examples.en: I don't know the detailed rules, so I just winged it.
